questions.questionText: The most common cause of primary postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is:
questions.optionB: Uterine atony (accounts for 70-80% of PPH — '4 T's: Tone, Tissue, Trauma, Thrombin')
questions.explanation: Primary PPH: blood loss >500mL (vaginal) or >1000mL (CS) within 24h of delivery. 4 T's: Tone (uterine atony — 70-80%, most common), Tissue (retained products/placenta), Trauma (cervical/vaginal tears, uterine rupture), Thrombin (coagulopathy/DIC). Management of atony: bimanual compression, uterotonic drugs (oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost/PGF2α, misoprostol), uterine tamponade (balloon), surgical (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy — last resort). PPH: leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide. Reference: DC Dutta — PPH.

questions.questionText: Puerperal sepsis is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) — also polymicrobial infections of the genital tract after delivery
questions.explanation: Puerperal sepsis: infection of genital tract after delivery. Most common organism: Group A β-hemolytic Streptococcus (S. pyogenes — historically 'childbed fever'). Also: E. coli, anaerobes, S. aureus, Group B Strep. Risk factors: prolonged labor, frequent vaginal examinations, CS, PROM, retained products. Features: fever >38°C, uterine tenderness, foul-smelling lochia, tachycardia. Treatment: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole). Prevention: aseptic technique, prophylactic antibiotics for CS. Reference: DC Dutta — Puerperal Sepsis.
